examples.explanation: Step 1: Generate all permutations of [0,1,2]: [0,1,2], [0,2,1], [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Step 2: Check [0,1,2] against edges: edge [0,1]: 0 at index 0, 1 at index 1 → valid. edge [1,2]: 1 at index 1, 2 at index 2 → valid. All edges valid → return [0,1,2].

Step 1: Generate permutations (first few): [0,1,2,3], [0,1,3,2], [0,2,1,3], etc. Step 2: Check [0,1,2,3]: edge [0,1]: 0 at 0, 1 at 1 → valid. edge [2,3]: 2 at 2, 3 at 3 → valid. Valid found → return [0,1,2,3].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of nodes and check each for topological order by verifying all edges go from earlier to later nodes. Use this only for tiny graphs to understand the problem, as it's extremely inefficient.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Topological Sort
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of nodes and validate each as a topological ordering
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves topological sorting by exhaustively checking every possible ordering of nodes. The intuition is that a topological order must satisfy all directed edges: for every edge (u→v), node u must appear before v in the sequence. Since there are n! possible permutations of n nodes, we generate them all and test each one against all edges. This works because topological ordering is essentially a linear arrangement respecting all precedence constraints, and by checking all permutations, we're guaranteed to find a valid one if it exists. The algorithm: 1) Generate all permutations of nodes using backtracking or library functions. 2) For each permutation, iterate through all edges to verify that for every edge (u→v), the index of u is less than the index of v in the permutation. 3) Return the first valid permutation found, or an empty list if none exists. Time complexity is O(n! * n * e) where e is the number of edges, but typically simplified to O(n! * n) since e ≤ n². Space complexity is O(n) for storing the current permutation and recursion stack. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) which is the permutation validation check. Common mistakes include not handling cycles properly (this algorithm will simply find no valid permutation for cyclic graphs), inefficient permutation generation, and not optimizing the validation check. Real code pattern: def topological_sort_brute(n, edges): permutations = generate_all_permutations(list(range(n))); for perm in permutations: if is_valid_topological(perm, edges): return perm; return [].

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate all permutations of [0,1,2]: [0,1,2], [0,2,1], [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Step 2: Check [0,1,2] against edges: edge [0,1]: 0 at index 0, 1 at index 1 → valid. edge [1,2]: 1 at index 1, 2 at index 2 → valid. All edges valid → return [0,1,2].

Step 1: Generate permutations (first few): [0,1,2,3], [0,1,3,2], [0,2,1,3], etc. Step 2: Check [0,1,2,3]: edge [0,1]: 0 at 0, 1 at 1 → valid. edge [2,3]: 2 at 2, 3 at 3 → valid. Valid found → return [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Validate Topological Order
ladders.desc: Check if a given permutation satisfies all edge constraints
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine validates whether a specific node ordering forms a valid topological sort. The intuition is that topological ordering requires all directed edges to point forward in the sequence. Given a permutation (candidate ordering) and a list of edges, we verify that for every edge (u→v), node u appears before v in the permutation. This works because if any edge points backward (u after v), the ordering violates the dependency. The algorithm: 1) Create a dictionary mapping each node to its index in the permutation for O(1) lookups. 2) Iterate through all edges. 3) For each edge (u,v), check if index[u] < index[v]. 4) If all edges satisfy this, return True; otherwise return False at first violation. Time complexity is O(n + e) where n is nodes (for building index map) and e is edges (for validation). Space complexity is O(n) for the index map. This feeds into L0 by being the validation check for each generated permutation. Common mistakes include not handling missing nodes in edges, using linear search instead of hash map for index lookup (making it O(n*e)), and not short-circuiting on first failure. Real code pattern: def is_valid_topological(perm, edges): index = {node: i for i, node in enumerate(perm)}; for u,v in edges: if index[u] >= index[v]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build index: {0:0, 1:1, 2:2}. Step 2: Check edge [0,1]: index[0]=0, index[1]=1 → 0<1 → valid. Step 3: Check edge [1,2]: index[1]=1, index[2]=2 → 1<2 → valid. All edges valid → return True.
Step 1: Build index: {2:0, 1:1, 0:2}. Step 2: Check edge [0,1]: index[0]=2, index[1]=1 → 2≥1 → invalid. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Permutation Generation with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orderings of nodes using recursive backtracking
ladders.explanation: This core logic generates all permutations of a set of nodes, which is essential for the brute force approach. The intuition is that a permutation is an arrangement of all elements in a specific order, and by systematically exploring all arrangements via recursion and backtracking, we can enumerate every possible ordering. This works because at each step, we choose an unused element to place in the current position, recurse to fill the next position, and then backtrack to try other choices. The algorithm: 1) Start with an empty current permutation and a set of unused nodes. 2) If the current permutation has length n (all nodes used), add it to the results. 3) Otherwise, for each unused node: add it to the current permutation, mark it as used, recurse, then remove it (backtrack) to try other nodes. Time complexity is O(n!) since there are n! permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and current permutation. This feeds into L1 by providing the permutations to validate. Common mistakes include not copying the current permutation before adding to results (leading to reference issues), inefficient unused node tracking (use boolean array or set), and not handling duplicates if nodes have identical labels. Real code pattern: def generate_permutations(nodes): result = []; def backtrack(curr, used): if len(curr)==len(nodes): result.append(curr[:]); return; for i in range(len(nodes)): if not used[i]: used[i]=True; curr.append(nodes[i]); backtrack(curr, used); curr.pop(); used[i]=False; backtrack([], [False]*len(nodes)); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start backtrack: curr=[], used=[F,F]. Step 2: First iteration i=0: used[0]=T, curr=[0]; recurse. Step 3: In recursion: curr=[0], used=[T,F]; i=1: used[1]=T, curr=[0,1]; recurse. Step 4: Base case: curr=[0,1] length 2 → add [0,1] to result; backtrack: pop 1, used[1]=F. Step 5: Back to step 2: pop 0, used[0]=F. Step 6: Second iteration i=1: used[1]=T, curr=[1]; recurse. Step 7: In recursion: curr=[1], used=[F,T]; i=0: used[0]=T, curr=[1,0]; recurse. Step 8: Base case: add [1,0]; backtrack. Final result: [[0,1],[1,0]].

Step 1: Generate permutations via backtracking. First path: choose 0, then 1, then 2 → [0,1,2]. Backtrack: after [0,1,2], try [0,2,1]. Continue: [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Total 6 permutations.

ladders.title: Building Block — Directed Graph Edge Validation
ladders.desc: Check if a sequence satisfies a single directed edge constraint
ladders.explanation: This building block checks whether two nodes appear in the correct order relative to a directed edge in a given sequence. The intuition is that for a directed edge (u→v) representing a dependency (u must come before v), we need to verify that u's position is before v's in the sequence. This works by comparing the indices of u and v: if index(u) < index(v), the edge is satisfied; otherwise, it's violated. This is a fundamental check that gets repeated for all edges in the validation sub-routine. The algorithm: 1) Given a sequence (list of nodes) and an edge (u,v). 2) Find the indices of u and v in the sequence (using linear search or precomputed map). 3) Return True if index_u < index_v, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(n) for linear search per edge, or O(1) with a precomputed index map. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the basic check used when validating a permutation against all edges. Common mistakes include assuming nodes are always in the sequence (handle missing nodes gracefully), using inefficient search methods, and not considering zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: def satisfies_edge(seq, u, v): try: return seq.index(u) < seq.index(v); except ValueError: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find index of 0 in [0,1,2] → 0. Step 2: Find index of 1 → 1. Step 3: Compare 0 < 1 → True.
Step 1: Find index of 1 in [2,0,1] → 2. Step 2: Find index of 2 → 0. Step 3: Compare 2 < 0 → False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Index of Element in List
ladders.desc: Locate the position of a given element in a sequence
ladders.explanation: This basic operation finds the index (position) of a specific element within a list or array. The intuition is that in many algorithms, including topological sort validation, we need to know where an element appears to compare positions. This works by scanning the list from start to end until the target element is found, then returning its position (usually 0-based). If the element is not found, we handle it appropriately (e.g., return -1 or raise an error). The algorithm: 1) Iterate through the list with an index counter. 2) At each position, compare the current element to the target. 3) If equal, return the current index. 4) If end of list reached without finding, return -1. Time complexity is O(n) for a list of length n. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 as the mechanism to get node indices for edge validation. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates (returns first occurrence), assuming 1-based indexing, and not checking for empty lists. Real code pattern: def find_index(lst, target): for i in range(len(lst)): if lst[i] == target: return i; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, lst[0]=0 ≠ 1 → continue. Step 2: i=1, lst[1]=1 == 1 → return 1.
Step 1: i=0, 5≠7. Step 2: i=1, 3≠7. Step 3: i=2, 7==7 → return 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — List Traversal and Comparison
ladders.desc: Iterate through a list and compare elements
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves basic operations on lists: traversing (looping through) elements and comparing them to other values. The intuition is that algorithms often require examining each element in a sequence to perform checks, searches, or transformations. This works by using a loop (e.g., for or while) to access each element one by one, and comparison operators (==, <, >, etc.) to evaluate conditions. For beginners, this is about understanding how to move through data structures step-by-step. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a loop index or use an iterator. 2) In each iteration, access the current element. 3) Compare it to a target or other elements as needed. 4) Perform actions based on the comparison. Time complexity depends on list length, typically O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L4 as the core skill needed to find an element's index. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, modifying the list while iterating, and not handling empty lists. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(lst)): current = lst[i]; if current == target: # do something.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, current=1, compare 1==2 → false. Step 2: i=1, current=2, compare 2==2 → true → print 'Found at index 1'.
Step 1: i=0, current=5, 5<20 → true, continue. Step 2: i=1, current=10, 10<20 → true, continue. Step 3: i=2, current=15, 15<20 → true, end loop → all elements satisfy → return True.

approaches.name: DFS with Stack O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS on the graph, pushing nodes onto a stack after visiting all their neighbors, ensuring dependencies are resolved first. This is the standard recursive DFS approach that builds the topological order in reverse.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Topological Sort Using DFS with Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of topological sort using DFS recursion and a stack to produce valid ordering.
ladders.explanation: Topological sort arranges nodes in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) such that for every directed edge u→v, node u comes before v in the ordering. This is essential for scheduling tasks with dependencies. The DFS with stack approach works by performing depth-first traversal: when we finish exploring all neighbors of a node (i.e., after recursive calls return), we push that node onto a stack. This ensures that nodes with no outgoing dependencies (or whose dependencies are already processed) are added last in recursion but appear first when the stack is popped, creating the correct topological order. The algorithm handles cycles by detecting them during DFS using a visited set with states (unvisited, visiting, visited). Intuition: Think of it as a post-order traversal where you only mark a task as 'done' after all its prerequisites are completed. Step-by-step: 1. Build adjacency list from edges. 2. Initialize visited dictionary with three states: 0=unvisited, 1=visiting (in current DFS path), 2=visited. 3. Define recursive DFS function that marks node as visiting, explores neighbors, and if all neighbors are processed without cycles, pushes node to stack and marks as visited. 4. Iterate through all nodes, calling DFS on unvisited ones. 5. After DFS completes for all nodes, pop stack to get topological order. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is visited once. Space complexity is O(V) for stack, visited dictionary, and recursion depth. Connection to L1: This level integrates the DFS subroutine with cycle detection and stack management. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs, forgetting to check for cycles, or incorrect stack usage. Real code pattern: def topological_sort(n, edges): adj = [[] for _ in range(n)], visited = [0]*n, stack = [], def dfs(u): if visited[u]==1: return False (cycle), if visited[u]==2: return True, visited[u]=1, for v in adj[u]: if not dfs(v): return False, visited[u]=2, stack.append(u), return True, for i in range(n): if visited[i]==0: if not dfs(i): return [] (cycle detected), return stack[::-1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0:[], 1:[0], 2:[0], 3:[1,2]. Step 2: Initialize visited all 0, stack empty. Step 3: Start DFS from node 0 (unvisited). visited[0]=1, neighbors none, push 0 to stack, visited[0]=2. Stack: [0]. Step 4: DFS from node 1: visited[1]=1, neighbor 0 (visited=2), push 1 to stack, visited[1]=2. Stack: [0,1]. Step 5: DFS from node 2: visited[2]=1, neighbor 0 (visited=2), push 2 to stack, visited[2]=2. Stack: [0,1,2]. Step 6: DFS from node 3: visited[3]=1, neighbors 1 and 2 (both visited=2), push 3 to stack, visited[3]=2. Stack: [0,1,2,3]. Step 7: Reverse stack: [3,2,1,0] → topological order [0,1,2,3] (or [0,2,1,3] depending on neighbor order).

Step 1: Adjacency list: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]. Step 2: visited all 0. Step 3: DFS from node 0: visited[0]=1, go to neighbor 1. Step 4: DFS node 1: visited[1]=1, go to neighbor 2. Step 5: DFS node 2: visited[2]=1, go to neighbor 0. Step 6: Node 0 is visiting (visited[0]=1), cycle detected, return False. Step 7: Unwind recursion, return [] as output.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS with Cycle Detection and Stack Push
ladders.desc: Recursive DFS function that detects cycles and pushes nodes to stack after processing neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of topological sort. It performs depth-first search on a node, ensuring that all its dependencies are resolved before the node itself is added to the ordering. The intuition is similar to completing tasks: you can only finish a task after all its prerequisites are done. The function uses a visited array with three states: 0 (unvisited), 1 (visiting - in the current DFS path), and 2 (visited - fully processed). Step-by-step: 1. If node is visiting (state 1), a cycle is detected—return False. 2. If node is visited (state 2), return True (already processed). 3. Mark node as visiting. 4. Recursively call DFS on all neighbors. If any returns False (cycle), propagate False. 5. After all neighbors are processed successfully, mark node as visited and push it onto a stack. 6. Return True. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(V+E) in total across all calls, but per call it depends on the node's edges. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth and visited states. Connection to L0: This function is called for each node in the main algorithm to build the stack. Common mistakes include not using three states for cycle detection, incorrect order of pushing to stack, or infinite recursion. Real code pattern: def dfs(u): if visited[u]==1: return False, if visited[u]==2: return True, visited[u]=1, for v in adj[u]: if not dfs(v): return False, visited[u]=2, stack.append(u), return True.

examples.input: Node 0 with neighbors [1,2], visited all 0, stack empty
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): visited[0]=0 → mark as visiting (1). Step 2: Process neighbor 1: dfs(1): visited[1]=0 → mark as visiting, neighbors none → mark as visited (2), push 1 to stack. Stack: [1]. Step 3: Process neighbor 2: dfs(2): similar, push 2 to stack. Stack: [1,2]. Step 4: Back to node 0: all neighbors done, mark as visited (2), push 0 to stack. Stack: [1,2,0]. Return True.

examples.input: Node 0 with neighbors [1], node 1 with neighbors [0], visited all 0
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): visited[0]=0 → mark as visiting. Step 2: Process neighbor 1: dfs(1): visited[1]=0 → mark as visiting. Step 3: Process neighbor of 1: neighbor 0 is visiting (state 1) → return False. Step 4: Propagate False back to dfs(0), return False.

testCases.input: node=0, neighbors=[1], visited=[0,0], but DFS on 1 returns False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Post-order DFS for Dependency Resolution
ladders.desc: Using DFS to process nodes after their dependencies, enabling reverse ordering via stack.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in a DAG, if you perform a depth-first search and only consider a node 'finished' after all its descendants are processed, then collecting nodes in post-order (after recursive calls) gives a reverse topological order. When you push nodes onto a stack after finishing their DFS, the last node finished (deepest in dependencies) goes to the bottom of the stack, and the first node finished (with no dependencies) goes to the top. Popping the stack reverses this, yielding the correct topological order. Intuition: Imagine a tree of tasks where leaves are independent tasks; you complete leaves first, then their parents. In DFS, you reach a leaf, push it, backtrack to parent, push parent after children are done. For DAGs, this generalizes with cycle checks. Step-by-step: 1. Start DFS from a node. 2. Recursively visit all neighbors. 3. After returning from all neighbor calls, push current node to stack. 4. Repeat for all nodes. This ensures that for any edge u→v, v is pushed before u (since DFS goes from u to v, and v finishes first), so after reversal, u comes before v. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space is O(V) for stack and recursion. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the DFS subroutine with the stack push. Common mistakes include pushing nodes pre-order or not using a stack. Real code pattern: In DFS, after for v in neighbors: dfs(v), do stack.append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): go to neighbor 1. Step 2: dfs(1): no neighbors, push 1 to stack. Stack: [1]. Step 3: Back to dfs(0): go to neighbor 2. Step 4: dfs(2): no neighbors, push 2 to stack. Stack: [1,2]. Step 5: Back to dfs(0): all neighbors done, push 0 to stack. Stack: [1,2,0]. Popping gives [0,2,1] or [0,1,2].

Step 1: dfs(0): go to neighbor 1. Step 2: dfs(1): go to neighbor 2. Step 3: dfs(2): no neighbors, push 2. Stack: [2]. Step 4: Back to dfs(1): push 1. Stack: [2,1]. Step 5: Back to dfs(0): push 0. Stack: [2,1,0]. Popping gives [0,1,2].

testCases.input: Graph: no edges, nodes 0,1,2, DFS from each

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Representing a directed graph and performing basic DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two parts: representing a graph and traversing it using DFS. For topological sort, we typically use an adjacency list: an array of lists where index u contains all neighbors v such that there is an edge u→v. This is efficient for sparse graphs with O(V+E) space. DFS traversal starts at a node, explores as deep as possible along edges before backtracking. Intuition: DFS mimics exploring a maze by going down one path until dead end, then returning to try other paths. Step-by-step: 1. Build adjacency list from list of edges. 2. Initialize visited set to track visited nodes. 3. Define recursive DFS function: mark node as visited, for each neighbor, if not visited, call DFS recursively. 4. Call DFS on starting node. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space is O(V) for visited and recursion depth. Connection to L2: This basic DFS is extended with post-order processing for topological sort. Common mistakes include incorrect adjacency list building (direction matters), not handling disconnected graphs, or infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)], for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v), visited = set(), def dfs(u): visited.add(u), for v in adj[u]: if v not in visited: dfs(v).

examples.output: Visited nodes in DFS order: 0,1,2 or 0,2,1
examples.trace: Step 1: Build adj: 0:[1,2], 1:[], 2:[]. Step 2: visited={}. Step 3: dfs(0): add 0 to visited, go to neighbor 1. Step 4: dfs(1): add 1 to visited, no neighbors, return. Step 5: Back to dfs(0): go to neighbor 2. Step 6: dfs(2): add 2 to visited, done. Visited: {0,1,2}.

Step 1: adj: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[3], 3:[]. Step 2: visited={}. Step 3: dfs(0): add 0, go to 1. Step 4: dfs(1): add 1, go to 2. Step 5: dfs(2): add 2, go to 3. Step 6: dfs(3): add 3, return. Visited: {0,1,2,3}.

testCases.expected: Visited={0,1} (cycle, but visited prevents infinite recursion)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop for Order Reversal
ladders.desc: Using a stack data structure to reverse the order of elements.
ladders.explanation: A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure where elements are added (pushed) and removed (popped) from the top. In topological sort, we use a stack to reverse the order in which nodes are processed during DFS. Intuition: If you complete tasks in a certain order and push each task onto a stack as you finish it, then when you pop them off, they come out in reverse order—like stacking plates: last plate placed is first removed. This reversal is key because DFS finishes dependent nodes (neighbors) before the node that depends on them, so pushing nodes post-order and then popping gives the correct topological order. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty stack. 2. During DFS, after processing all neighbors, push the current node onto the stack. 3. After DFS completes for all nodes, pop elements from the stack to get the topological order. Time complexity for stack operations is O(1) per push/pop, O(V) total. Space is O(V) for the stack. Connection to L3: This operation is integrated into DFS traversal. Common mistakes include pushing nodes pre-order, not using a stack (e.g., using a list without reversal), or incorrect popping order. Real code pattern: stack = [], def dfs(u): ... stack.append(u), ... result = [], while stack: result.append(stack.pop()).

examples.input: Push nodes 1,2,3 in that order, then pop
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push 1 → stack=[1]. Step 3: push 2 → stack=[1,2]. Step 4: push 3 → stack=[1,2,3]. Step 5: pop → returns 3, stack=[1,2]. Step 6: pop → returns 2, stack=[1]. Step 7: pop → returns 1, stack=[].

examples.input: Push nodes in order of DFS finish: A, B, C
examples.trace: Step 1: DFS finishes A first, push A → stack=[A]. Step 2: DFS finishes B, push B → stack=[A,B]. Step 3: DFS finishes C, push C → stack=[A,B,C]. Step 4: Pop all: C, then B, then A.

testCases.input: Push a,b,c in that order, pop twice
Push x, push y, pop, push z, pop all
testCases.expected: First pop: y, then pops: z,x

testCases.input: Push in order: node1, node2, node3, then reverse via pop
Stack with elements [a,b], push c, pop
Push elements from list [10,20,30], pop all
Initialize stack, no pushes, check if empty

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Dependencies
ladders.desc: Understanding directed graphs and how they represent dependencies between items.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows) between them. Each edge has a direction: from a source node to a target node. This naturally models dependencies: if there is an edge from node A to node B, it means A must come before B (A depends on B, or B is a prerequisite of A). For example, in course scheduling, if course A requires course B, we have an edge B→A. A directed acyclic graph (DAG) is a directed graph with no cycles, meaning you cannot start at a node and follow edges to return to it. DAGs are essential for topological sort because cycles create circular dependencies that make ordering impossible. Intuition: Think of a recipe: you need to chop vegetables (node B) before cooking them (node A), so B→A; but if A required B and B required A, you'd have a cycle and couldn't start. Step-by-step: 1. Identify items as nodes. 2. For each dependency "X before Y", add directed edge X→Y. 3. Ensure no cycles (for topological sort). Time and space depend on graph size, but conceptually, it's about understanding relationships. Connection to L4: This foundation is needed to build graphs for DFS and stack operations. Common mistakes include confusing edge direction or not recognizing cycles. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code—e.g., edges represent prerequisites.

examples.input: Tasks: A depends on B, B depends on C
examples.output: Graph: nodes A,B,C, edges: B→A, C→B
examples.trace: Step 1: Node A requires B, so edge from B to A (B must come before A). Step 2: Node B requires C, so edge from C to B. Step 3: Visualize: C → B → A. This is a DAG with order C,B,A.

examples.input: Courses: Math before Calculus, Calculus before Physics, Physics before Math
examples.trace: Step 1: Math before Calculus: edge Math→Calculus. Step 2: Calculus before Physics: edge Calculus→Physics. Step 3: Physics before Math: edge Physics→Math. Step 4: This creates a cycle: Math→Calculus→Physics→Math, so not a DAG.

testCases.input: X before Y, Y before Z
P before Q, Q before R, R before P

testCases.expected: Graph with nodes only, no edges

testCases.input: M before N, N before O, O before P
A depends on B, B depends on C, C depends on A
Task1 before Task2, Task2 before Task3, Task1 before Task3

testCases.expected: Graph with node X, no edges

testCases.input: U before V, V before W, W before U
Alpha before Beta, Beta before Gamma, Gamma before Delta

approaches.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS) O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Use BFS by repeatedly removing nodes with zero indegree and updating indegrees of neighbors. This iteratively builds the topological order from sources without recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Topological Sort Using Kahn's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Kahn's Algorithm for topological sorting of a directed acyclic graph.
ladders.explanation: Kahn's Algorithm provides an iterative BFS-based approach to topological sorting by repeatedly removing nodes with zero indegree. The intuition is that in a DAG, there must be at least one node with no incoming edges (a source). By removing sources and updating indegrees of their neighbors, we systematically build a valid topological order. This approach avoids recursion and is particularly useful for detecting cycles. The algorithm begins by computing indegrees for all nodes and initializing a queue with nodes having indegree 0. While the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node, add it to the topological order, and decrement the indegree of its neighbors. If any neighbor's indegree becomes 0, it is enqueued. If the final order contains all nodes, we have a valid topological sort; otherwise, a cycle exists. Time complexity is O(V + E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for storing indegrees and the queue. This connects to the broader problem of topological sorting by providing an efficient, non-recursive solution that handles cycle detection gracefully. Common mistakes include forgetting to update indegrees correctly, not handling disconnected graphs, and missing cycle detection. Real code pattern involves adjacency list representation, indegree array, queue, and result list.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute indegrees: node0:0, node1:1, node2:1, node3:2. Step 2: Initialize queue with node0 (indegree 0). Step 3: Dequeue node0, add to order: [0]. Update neighbors: node1 indegree becomes 0, enqueue; node2 indegree becomes 0, enqueue. Step 4: Dequeue node1, add to order: [0,1]. Update neighbor node3: indegree becomes 1. Step 5: Dequeue node2, add to order: [0,1,2]. Update neighbor node3: indegree becomes 0, enqueue. Step 6: Dequeue node3, add to order: [0,1,2,3]. Step 7: Queue empty, order has 4 nodes = total nodes, valid topological sort.

Step 1: Compute indegrees: node0:1, node1:1, node2:1. Step 2: No node with indegree 0, queue remains empty. Step 3: Algorithm terminates with empty order. Step 4: Order length 0 < total nodes 3, cycle detected.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Indegree Computation and Queue Initialization
ladders.desc: Compute indegrees for all nodes and initialize queue with zero-indegree nodes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the foundation of Kahn's Algorithm, responsible for identifying starting points (sources) for topological sorting. The intuition is that topological order begins with nodes having no dependencies (indegree 0). By computing indegrees—the count of incoming edges to each node—we can locate these sources efficiently. The algorithm iterates through all edges in the graph, incrementing the indegree of the destination node for each edge. After computing indegrees, we scan all nodes to enqueue those with indegree 0. This step ensures that the BFS process starts from valid sources. Time complexity is O(V + E) as we traverse all nodes and edges. Space complexity is O(V) for storing indegrees. This feeds into the full problem by providing the initial state for the BFS traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect indegree updates for self-loops, missing nodes with no edges, and using inefficient data structures. Real code pattern involves an indegree array initialized to 0, adjacency list traversal, and queue initialization.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize indegrees array of size 4 to zeros. Step 2: Process edge 0->1: increment indegree[1] to 1. Step 3: Process edge 0->2: increment indegree[2] to 1. Step 4: Process edge 1->3: increment indegree[3] to 1. Step 5: Process edge 2->3: increment indegree[3] to 2. Step 6: Scan nodes: node0 indegree 0, enqueue; others have indegree >0.

Step 1: Initialize indegrees array of size 3 to zeros. Step 2: Process edge 0->1: increment indegree[1] to 1. Step 3: Process edge 1->2: increment indegree[2] to 1. Step 4: Process edge 2->0: increment indegree[0] to 1. Step 5: Scan nodes: all indegrees are 1, no node with indegree 0, queue remains empty.

ladders.title: Core Logic — BFS Traversal with Indegree Updates
ladders.desc: Process nodes in BFS order, updating indegrees of neighbors as nodes are removed.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the iterative heart of Kahn's Algorithm. The intuition is that by removing a source node (indegree 0), we effectively satisfy its dependencies, allowing its neighbors to potentially become new sources. The algorithm uses a queue to process nodes in BFS order. For each dequeued node, we add it to the topological order and iterate through its neighbors. For each neighbor, we decrement its indegree by 1 (simulating the removal of the incoming edge). If a neighbor's indegree becomes 0, it is enqueued as a new source. This process continues until the queue is empty. The key insight is that this systematically reduces the graph, maintaining the invariant that only nodes with no remaining dependencies are processed. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(V + E) as each node is dequeued once and each edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue. This connects to the key sub-routine by consuming the initialized queue and indegrees to build the order. Common mistakes include not checking for indegree becoming 0 before enqueueing, modifying indegrees incorrectly, and using stack instead of queue for BFS. Real code pattern involves while loop with queue, neighbor iteration, and indegree updates.

examples.trace: Step 1: Dequeue 0, order = [0]. Update neighbors: node1 indegree from 1 to 0, enqueue; node2 indegree from 1 to 0, enqueue. Step 2: Dequeue 1, order = [0,1]. Update neighbor node3: indegree from 2 to 1. Step 3: Dequeue 2, order = [0,1,2]. Update neighbor node3: indegree from 1 to 0, enqueue. Step 4: Dequeue 3, order = [0,1,2,3]. Step 5: Queue empty, done.

Step 1: Dequeue 0, order = [0]. Update neighbor node1: indegree from 1 to 0, enqueue. Step 2: Dequeue 1, order = [0,1]. Update neighbor node2: indegree from 1 to 0, enqueue. Step 3: Dequeue 2, order = [0,1,2]. Step 4: Queue empty, done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation with Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph using adjacency list for efficient neighbor access.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for implementing Kahn's Algorithm efficiently. The intuition is that topological sorting requires frequent access to a node's outgoing neighbors, which an adjacency list provides in O(1) average time. An adjacency list represents a graph as a dictionary or array of lists, where each key/index corresponds to a node, and its value is a list of neighboring nodes reachable via outgoing edges. This representation is space-efficient (O(V + E)) and allows quick iteration over neighbors. For Kahn's Algorithm, we need both the adjacency list (for traversing edges) and potentially a reverse adjacency list for indegree computation, though indegrees can be computed by scanning the forward list. The step-by-step process involves initializing an empty list for each node, then adding edges by appending destination nodes to the source's list. Time complexity for construction is O(E), and space is O(V + E). This connects to core logic by providing the data structure for neighbor iteration during BFS. Common mistakes include not handling nodes with no edges, incorrect indexing for numeric nodes, and using adjacency matrix which is inefficient for sparse graphs. Real code pattern uses dictionary with lists or list of lists.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph with 4 empty lists: {0:[], 1:[], 2:[], 3:[]}. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): append 1 to list of 0 → {0:[1], 1:[], 2:[], 3:[]}. Step 3: Add edge (0,2): append 2 to list of 0 → {0:[1,2], 1:[], 2:[], 3:[]}. Step 4: Add edge (1,3): append 3 to list of 1 → {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[], 3:[]}. Step 5: Add edge (2,3): append 3 to list of 2 → {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[3], 3:[]}.

Step 1: Initialize graph with 3 empty lists: {0:[], 1:[], 2:[]}. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): append 1 to list of 0 → {0:[1], 1:[], 2:[]}. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): append 2 to list of 1 → {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]}. Step 4: Add edge (2,0): append 0 to list of 2 → {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement queue operations (enqueue, dequeue) to manage node processing order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to BFS traversal in Kahn's Algorithm. The intuition is that a queue ensures nodes are processed in First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, which corresponds to level-by-level exploration in BFS. This is crucial for topological sorting as it processes sources in the order they become available, though any order of sources is valid. The queue supports two primary operations: enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front). In Kahn's Algorithm, we enqueue nodes when their indegree becomes 0 and dequeue to process them. Implementation can use collections.deque in Python for O(1) operations. The step-by-step involves initializing an empty queue, enqueueing initial sources, then in a loop: dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue new sources. Time complexity for each operation is O(1), and overall BFS uses O(V) queue operations. Space complexity is O(V) for queue storage. This connects to building block by providing the mechanism to traverse the adjacency list in BFS order. Common mistakes include using list for queue (which has O(n) dequeue), not checking queue emptiness, and confusing queue with stack. Real code pattern uses deque from collections.

examples.input: Initial queue = [0], operations: dequeue, enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: Start queue = [0]. Step 2: Dequeue → returns 0, queue = []. Step 3: Enqueue 1 → queue = [1]. Step 4: Enqueue 2 → queue = [1, 2]. Step 5: Dequeue → returns 1, queue = [2]. Step 6: Dequeue → returns 2, queue = [].

examples.input: Initial queue = [], operations: enqueue 0, enqueue 1, dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: Start queue = []. Step 2: Enqueue 0 → queue = [0]. Step 3: Enqueue 1 → queue = [0, 1]. Step 4: Dequeue → returns 0, queue = [1]. Step 5: Enqueue 2 → queue = [1, 2]. Step 6: Dequeue → returns 1, queue = [2]. Step 7: Dequeue → returns 2, queue = [].

testCases.input: Initial queue = [0, 1, 2], operations: dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue = [0], operations: dequeue, enqueue 1, dequeue
Initial queue = [], operations: enqueue 0, enqueue 1, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue = [0], operations: dequeue, enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue = [0, 1], operations: dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue = [], operations: enqueue 0, dequeue, enqueue 1, dequeue
Initial queue = [0, 1], operations: dequeue, dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue
Initial queue = [], operations: enqueue 0, enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue = [0], operations: dequeue, enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue, dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Indegree
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs and indegree as count of incoming edges to a node.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces directed graphs and indegree, which are prerequisites for topological sorting. A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges with direction, indicating a relationship from source to destination. Indegree of a node is the number of edges coming into it, representing dependencies or prerequisites. For example, in a course prerequisite graph, an edge from course A to B means A must be taken before B, so B's indegree increases. The intuition is that nodes with indegree 0 have no dependencies and can be processed first. To compute indegree, initialize a counter for each node to 0, then for each edge (u,v), increment indegree of v by 1. This is a linear scan over edges. Time complexity is O(E), space is O(V). This connects to basic operation by providing the metric used to decide when to enqueue nodes. Common mistakes include confusing indegree with outdegree, not handling self-loops correctly, and assuming undirected graphs. Real code pattern uses an array or dictionary to store indegrees.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize indegrees array [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge (0,1): increment indegree of node1 → [0,1,0,0]. Step 3: Edge (0,2): increment indegree of node2 → [0,1,1,0]. Step 4: Edge (1,3): increment indegree of node3 → [0,1,1,1]. Step 5: Edge (2,3): increment indegree of node3 → [0,1,1,2].

Step 1: Initialize indegrees array [0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge (0,1): increment indegree of node1 → [0,1,0]. Step 3: Edge (1,2): increment indegree of node2 → [0,1,1]. Step 4: Edge (2,0): increment indegree of node0 → [1,1,1].
